src:source.src:title: Use XML tags to structure your prompts
src:source.src:overview: Anthropic's official guidance on using XML tags with Claude. Recommends wrapping distinct content types in XML tags to reduce misinterpretation, especially for complex prompts mixing instructions, context, examples, and variable inputs.
src:source.src:author: Anthropic

pr:section.pr:title: Plan Layer
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Implementation plans with itemized steps, testable acceptance criteria, and a witness model for verification.
pr:section.pr:p: A specification describes what a system should be. Code implements it. But between specification and code lies a gap: the sequence of work that transforms intent into artifact. Plans bridge this gap. They decompose a specification into discrete, ordered steps with testable acceptance criteria that define when each step is done.

LLMs already produce plans as part of their workflow: when asked to implement a feature, they propose a sequence of steps. The plan layer channels these proposals into structured, validated XML. As Anthropic's prompt engineering guidance documents, wrapping distinct content types in XML tags reduces misinterpretation. Plans expressed as XML elements rather than free-text markdown benefit from the same delineation: each item, criterion, and witness is a distinct, addressable node that tooling can validate, query, and track.

Plans are self-contained. Unlike dec:decision elements which reference a separate pr:section for narrative context, plans embed their own title, overview, and item descriptions directly. This eliminates the need for hash-tracking between a plan and its narrative, and keeps plan content atomic: a single pln:plan element holds everything needed to understand and execute the plan. For rich content beyond simple strings, plans and items support embedding prose layer elements (pr:section, pr:p, trm:ref, src:cite) via xs:any for open-world extensibility.

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding how the plan layer bridges specification and implementation through structured, testable plans with self-contained content and embedded prose support.

pr:section.pr:title: Acceptance Criteria and Witnesses
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Testable criteria with structured evidence for verification.
pr:section.pr:p: Each plan item carries an acceptance block containing one or more criteria. A criterion is mixed content: inline text describes what must be true. The optional ref attribute links the criterion to the spec node it verifies, creating a traceable connection between the plan and the specification.
Witnesses are the evidence that proves a criterion is met. Each criterion can have zero or more witness children, classified by type:

pr:ul.pr:li: command: a shell command whose exit code indicates pass (0) or fail (non-zero). The simplest form of automated verification.
script: an executable script with an optional lang attribute specifying the language. For verification logic that exceeds a single command.
manual: a human verification step with an optional role attribute specifying who performs it. For criteria that cannot be automated (security reviews, UX assessments).

pr:section.pr:p: The witness type enumeration is extensible, following the same pattern as extensible relation types and source types. Extension layers define new witness types annotated with pln:witness-type-properties, activated via a rel:relation type="refines" edge to type-witness-kind.

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding how the witness model structures verification evidence for plan acceptance criteria, with extensible witness types.

pr:section.pr:title: Plan Revision Chains
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Succession chains for plan evolution using the extensible relation type pattern.
pr:section.pr:p: Plans evolve. An initial plan may prove incomplete or misguided after implementation begins. Rather than editing the original plan (which would lose the history of what was originally proposed), a new plan can revise the old one. The pln:revises element records this succession: its from attribute names the new plan, and its to attribute names the old one.

The revises element is an extension relation type following the same pattern as dec:supersedes in the decision layer. It carries a rel:relation-type-properties annotation declaring it as acyclic (a plan cannot transitively revise itself) and is activated via a rel:relation type="refines" edge from type-revises to type-relation-kind.

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding how pln:revises creates succession chains for plan evolution, parallel to dec:supersedes for decision succession.

pr:section.pr:title: Creating and Managing Plans
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Practical guide to authoring plans with acceptance criteria and witnesses.
pr:section.pr:p: A plan starts with a title, an optional overview, and a status of proposed. Add items for each discrete step. Each item needs a title, optional description, at least one acceptance criterion, and a status of pending.

For item ordering and dependencies, use existing relation types: rel:relation type="depends-on" when one item cannot start until another completes, and rel:relation type="precedes" for sequencing without hard dependency.

When simple string fields are insufficient, embed prose layer elements directly inside the plan or its items. A pr:section inside a plan provides a rich overview; a pr:p inside an item provides a rich description with terminology references and source citations.

Attach witnesses to criteria for verification. Use command witnesses for simple shell checks, script witnesses for complex verification logic, and manual witnesses for human sign-off requirements. A criterion with no witnesses is still valid but relies on human judgment alone.
When a plan needs revision, create a new plan and link it with pln:revises. Set the old plan's status to abandoned and the new plan's status to proposed or active.

org:task.org:purpose: Step-by-step guide for authoring plans with acceptance criteria, witnesses, embedded prose, and revision chains.

rel:relation.rel:note: The plan layer adds implementation planning as a semantic dimension within the layered architecture.

trm:term.trm:name: PlanType
trm:term.trm:definition: The XSD complexType that defines the structure of plan elements in the plan layer.

trm:term.trm:name: RevisesType
trm:term.trm:definition: The XSD complexType that defines the structure of revision chains in the plan layer.

trm:term.trm:name: WitnessKind
trm:term.trm:definition: The XSD simpleType that enumerates witness type values for plan acceptance criteria.

rel:relation.rel:note: Activates the revises extension relation type defined in plan.xsd, enabling plan revision chains in the connectivity graph.
The PlanType complexType (id="type-plan") is the schema definition for plan elements in the plan layer.
The RevisesType complexType (id="type-revises") is the schema definition for revision chains in the plan layer.
The WitnessKind simpleType (id="type-witness-kind") is the schema definition for witness type classification in the plan layer.
The plan layer's revises element is an extension relation type, depending on the extensible relation type mechanism for activation and graph integration.
Acceptance criteria and witnesses are a core component of the plan layer.
Revision chains are the plan layer's mechanism for plan evolution.
Usage guide for authoring plans within the plan layer.

spec:clayers.llm:node: Describes the plan layer: implementation plans with itemized steps and testable acceptance criteria. Plans are self-contained (embed their own title, overview, items) unlike decisions (which reference external prose). Plans and items support xs:any for embedding rich prose layer elements. The witness model defines how criteria are verified: commands, scripts, and manual human checks. The pln:revises element is an extension relation type for plan succession chains, activated via a refines edge to type-relation-kind.

Describes the acceptance model: criteria define what must be true, witnesses provide evidence. Three witness types: command (shell exit code), script (executable with lang attribute), manual (human with role attribute). The witness type enumeration is extensible via the same pattern as relation types and source types.

Describes plan revision chains: pln:revises links a new plan to the old plan it replaces. Follows the same extension relation type pattern as dec:supersedes. Acyclic: a plan cannot transitively revise itself.

Practical guide for authoring plans. Covers: creating plans with title and overview, adding items with acceptance criteria and witnesses, using rel:relation for item ordering (depends-on, precedes), embedding prose layer elements for rich content, and creating revision chains.

External source: Anthropic's official documentation on using XML tags to structure prompts. Recommends XML delineation for complex prompts to reduce misinterpretation. Cited as motivation for the plan layer's structured XML approach.
spec:clayers.llm:schema: The plan schema captures implementation plans as structured metadata. Each <pln:plan> is self-contained with an embedded title, optional overview, status, and one or more items. Items have titles, optional descriptions, acceptance criteria with witnesses, and status tracking. Plans and items support xs:any for embedding prose layer elements (pr:section, pr:p, trm:ref, src:cite). The witness model: command (shell exit code), script (executable with lang), manual (human with role). The <pln:revises> element is an extension relation type for plan revision chains, activated by a refines edge to type-relation-kind.

A self-contained implementation plan. The id makes it a node. Contains embedded title, optional overview, status (proposed/active/completed/ abandoned), optional embedded prose via xs:any, and one or more items. Unlike dec:decision, plans do NOT reference external prose via ref.

An extension relation type for plan revision chains. The from attribute names the new plan, the to attribute names the old one. Acyclic: a plan cannot transitively revise itself. Must be activated by a refines edge from type-revises to type-relation-kind in spec content.
spec:clayers.art:exempt: Conceptual documentation describing the plan layer's purpose and design. No code implements this concept.
Conceptual documentation describing the acceptance criteria and witness model. No code implements this concept.
Conceptual documentation describing plan revision chains. No code implements this concept.
Task documentation for authoring plans. No code implements this task.
External source declaration for Anthropic XML tags documentation. Bibliography entry, not a code-mapped node.

art:mapping.art:note: PlanType complexType: title, overview, status, item children.
RevisesType complexType: ref attribute linking to revised plan.
WitnessKind simpleType: command, script, manual witness types.
Schema header, plan and item element declarations.
PlanType, ItemType complexTypes with status and content model.
AcceptanceType and CriterionType complex type definitions.
WitnessType, WitnessKind definitions for plan acceptance criteria witnesses.
RevisesType extension relation type and remaining type definitions.
AcceptanceType, CriterionType, WitnessType: structured acceptance criteria with typed witnesses (command, script, manual).
Source reference to Anthropic XML tags guide in plans.xml.
